framework: Go Idiomatic — Idiomatic Go Code Checklist

scoring.globalScore: average of all applicable rules across every criterion, 0-100

linterLegend.govet: go vet — suspicious constructs (copylocks, printf, composites, lostcancel)
linterLegend.errname: errname — Err prefix / Error suffix on errors
linterLegend.nakedret: nakedret — naked returns in long functions

dimensions.name: Formatting & Tooling
dimensions.summary: Mechanical style handled by Go's own tools.

rules.text: Code is gofmt/gofumpt-clean (no manual alignment, tabs for indentation).
Imports are grouped (stdlib, then third-party) and ordered via goimports.
Lines break on semantics rather than an arbitrary hard column; no needlessly long lines.
Identifiers use MixedCaps/mixedCaps, never ALL_CAPS or snake_case.

dimensions.name: Naming
dimensions.summary: Names are short, consistent, and follow Go conventions.

rules.text: Initialisms keep consistent case (URL, ID, HTTP — not Url, Id, Http).
Package names are short, lower-case, no underscores, singular, and not stuttered by callers.
Method receivers use a 1–2 letter abbreviation, consistent across the type (not 'self'/'this'/'me').
Variable names are short in small scopes (i, c, r) and more descriptive only at package scope.
Getters omit the 'Get' prefix (owner.Owner(), not owner.GetOwner()).
Single-method interfaces are named with the -er suffix (Reader, Formatter) where natural.
Names don't stutter with their package (http.Server, not http.HTTPServer).

dimensions.name: Comments & Documentation
dimensions.summary: Doc comments are present and well-formed.

rules.text: Every exported name has a doc comment beginning with the name and ending with a period.
Each package has a package comment adjacent to the package clause (no blank line).
Comments are full sentences; they explain why, not restate the obvious what.

dimensions.name: Error Handling
dimensions.summary: Errors are values: returned, wrapped, and checked — never exceptions.

rules.text: All returned errors are checked; none are silently discarded with '_'.
Normal failure paths return errors rather than calling panic.
Error strings are lowercase and not ended with punctuation.
Wrapped errors use fmt.Errorf with %w; comparisons use errors.Is/errors.As (not ==/type-switch on strings).
Sentinel errors are exported as ErrXxx; custom error types are named XxxError.
Each error is handled once — not logged and returned at the same level.
Validity is signaled by a returned (value, ok) or (value, error), not by in-band sentinels like -1 or "".
Error flow is handled first with an early return, keeping the happy path un-indented.

dimensions.name: Control Flow & Readability
dimensions.summary: Shallow nesting, early returns, minimal ceremony.

rules.text: No 'else' after an if-branch that already returns/continues/breaks.
Nesting stays shallow; guard clauses and continue are used instead of deep if-pyramids.
Naked returns appear only in very short functions, not medium/long ones.
Named result parameters are used only when they aid clarity, not as redundant noise.

dimensions.name: Interfaces & Types
dimensions.summary: Accept interfaces, return structs; keep interfaces small and consumer-defined.

rules.text: Functions accept interfaces and return concrete types; they don't return interfaces by default.
Interfaces are declared in the consumer package that uses them, not the producer.
Interfaces are small (often one method); no broad 'everything' interfaces.
Code passes interface values, never pointers to interfaces.
Receiver type (pointer vs value) is consistent per type; pointer used for mutation, large structs, or sync fields.
Type assertions use the comma-ok form (v, ok := x.(T)); single-value assertions are avoided.
Compile-time interface checks use 'var _ Iface = (*T)(nil)' where helpful.

dimensions.name: Concurrency
dimensions.summary: Clear goroutine lifetimes and disciplined channel/context use.

rules.text: Every goroutine has a clear, documented exit condition; no fire-and-forget leaks.
APIs are synchronous by default, letting callers add concurrency.
Channels are unbuffered by default; buffer sizes >1 are justified.
context.Context is the first parameter and is not stored in a struct field.
sync.Mutex/RWMutex are zero-value, non-pointer fields and are never copied.
Slices/maps are copied at trust boundaries before storing or returning internal state.

dimensions.name: Structs & Initialization
dimensions.summary: Clear, keyed initialization and purposeful embedding.

rules.text: Struct literals use field names; positional (unkeyed) literals are avoided.
Zero-value fields are omitted from struct literals.
Embedding is used only for real composition; embedded fields sit at the top of the field list.
Flexible constructors use functional options rather than many optional parameters or telescoping constructors.
Enums start iota at 1 (or intentionally use the zero value) so an unset value is detectable.
Exhaustive switches over enum-like types handle all cases.

dimensions.name: Packages & Globals
dimensions.summary: Healthy package boundaries and no hidden global state.

rules.text: No mutable package-level globals; shared state is passed via dependency injection.
init() is avoided or kept trivial, deterministic, and side-effect free.
os.Exit / log.Fatal are called only in main(), not in library code.
Blank imports (import _) appear only in main or test packages; dot imports are avoided.
Predeclared identifiers (error, string, len, new…) are not shadowed.

dimensions.name: Data, Slices & Stdlib
dimensions.summary: Idiomatic use of slices, maps, and the standard library.

rules.text: Empty slices are declared as 'var s []T' (nil), and emptiness is checked with len(s) == 0.
Slices/maps with a known size are preallocated with a capacity hint via make().
Primitive conversions prefer strconv over fmt.Sprint for performance-sensitive code.
Time is modeled with time.Time / time.Duration, not raw ints; no 24h/365d assumptions.
Cryptographic randomness uses crypto/rand, never math/rand.
defer is used for cleanup (Close/Unlock) right after acquiring the resource.
Parametric tests use the table-driven pattern with named subtests (t.Run).
Test failures print input, got, and want — not just 'failed'.
Public, non-trivial APIs have runnable Example functions.

dimensions.name: Non-Idiomatic Patterns (Java/C#-isms)
dimensions.summary: OOP-from-other-languages habits that read as un-Go. The flagship dimension for this skill.

rules.text: Interfaces are NOT prefixed with 'I' (Store, not IStore).
No Abstract*/Base*/*Impl types emulating class inheritance; behavior is composed.
No exception-style control flow: panic/recover is not used in place of returning errors.
No getter/setter pairs over plain data — export the field instead.
Construction uses NewX functions (and functional options); no Factory/Builder 'classes' where those suffice.
Constants use MixedCaps (MaxRetries), not Java/C# ALL_CAPS (MAX_RETRIES).
Packages aren't catch-all 'manager/service/util/helpers/common/base' dumping grounds named after layers.
Optional/absent values use zero values, (T, ok), or (T, error) — not pointers-to-everything to mimic nullable types.
Stateless behavior is package-level functions, not methods on an empty 'service'/'helper' struct used as a class.
Functions don't return interfaces 'for flexibility'; they return concrete types (accept interfaces, return structs).
Deep embedding is not used to simulate subclass hierarchies; composition stays shallow and explicit.
Error handling doesn't build large exception class hierarchies; it uses sentinel errors + wrapping + errors.Is/As.
